data.title: EU to examine bids for Warner Bros. submitted by Netflix, Paramount simultaneously
data.description:
European competition watchdogs are set to examine bids submitted by Netflix Inc. (NFLX) and Paramount Skydance Corp. (PSKY) to acquire the coveted assets of Warner Bros. Discovery Inc. (WBD) at the same time, according to a Bloomberg report from Wednesday, citing sources.

The report said the parallel examinations of the bids are now inevitable due to the timing of the rival proposals and the fact that both sides have already sounded out EU watchdogs about their plans.

Sources who spoke to Bloomberg said that a double probe would give Brussels unusual leverage over Warner’s destiny, as they could swiftly clear the deal for one bidder and launch a more in-depth investigation for the other, thus paving a path for a frontrunner to emerge eventually.

While Netflix is expected to win the bidding war for Warner's assets, its $82.7B all-cash deal is considered more complicated from an antitrust perspective due to the streaming giant’s powerful position in subscription video-on-demand across Europe.

On the other hand, Paramount recently held talks with French President Emmanuel Macron and held discussions with UK officials as part of a European charm offensive to gain support for its $108.4B hostile bid for Warner Bros.

data.title: Ray Dalio flags growing shift away from U.S. assets
data.description:
Ray Dalio, founder of Bridgewater Associates, said there has been an ongoing general diversification away from U.S. assets, particularly by global central banks.

Speaking to Bloomberg Television’s Francine Lacqua in Davos on Thursday, Dalio pointed to the sharp rise in gold prices as evidence of the shift. “When you look at gold being up 67%, it’s not a precious metal that goes up 60%,” he said. “It was bought by central banks particularly, but others, and in order to diversify fiat currencies, not just dollars.”

Dalio also said markets are paying too much attention to the trade war and not enough to what he described as a “capital war.” Referring to U.S. President Donald Trump ruling out military force in his push to acquire Greenland, Dalio said the remarks were significant. “That speech was very important because there is a line, red line,” he said, adding that any military move would carry major capital-market consequences.

“If there’s a military move, it has capital-war implications,” Dalio added.

The move away from U.S. assets has gained momentum amid rising transatlantic tensions and concerns over a potential weakening of NATO. Danish pension fund AkademikerPension has said it plans to exit U.S. Treasuries by the end of the month, citing credit risks.

“The US is basically not a good credit and long-term the US government finances are not sustainable,” Anders Schelde, chief investment officer at AkademikerPension, told Bloomberg on Tuesday. AkademikerPension, which manages around $25B in savings for academics, held about $100M in U.S. Treasuries at the end of 2025, Schelde said.

UBS Group chief executive officer Sergio Ermotti, however, has warned that the temptation to weaponize holdings of U.S. government debt is a “dangerous bet.”

U.S. Treasuries: U.S. 2-Year Bond Yield (US2Y), U.S. 5-Year (US5Y), U.S. 10-Year (US10Y), U.S. 30-Year (US30Y). Investors can also track bond ETFs here.

Major U.S. stock indices (sensitive to debt/economic concerns): (SPY), (QQQ), (DIA). Volatility indicator (VIX).

data.title: Texas Capital Bancshares GAAP EPS of $2.12 beats by $0.35, revenue of $327.48M beats by $4.19M
data.description:
• Texas Capital Bancshares press release (TCBI): Q4 GAAP EPS of $2.12 beats by $0.35.
  • Revenue of $327.48M (+15.4% Y/Y) beats by $4.19M.
  • Reaching record-levels, Book Value and Tangible Book Value(4) per share both increased 13% year-over-year
  • Capital ratios continue to be strong, achieving 12.1% CET1 and 16.1% Total Capital
  • Provision for credit losses for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $11.0 million, compared to $12.0 million for the third quarter of 2025. The $11.0 million provision for credit losses recorded in the fourth quarter of 2025 resulted primarily from an increase in criticized loans and $10.7 million in net charge-offs.

data.title: Commerce Bancshares GAAP EPS of $1.01 beats by $0.02, revenue of $449.36M beats by $6.74M
data.description:
• Commerce Bancshares press release (CBSH): Q4 GAAP EPS of $1.01 beats by $0.02.
  • Revenue of $449.36M (+6.5% Y/Y) beats by $6.74M.
  • Net interest income was $283.2 million, a $3.7 million increase over the prior quarter. The net yield on interest earning assets decreased four basis points to 3.60%.
  • Non-interest income totaled $166.2 million, an increase of $10.8 million, or 6.9%, over the same quarter last year.
  • The ratio of annualized net loan charge-offs to average loans was .22% in the current quarter compared to .23% in the prior quarter.
  • For the quarter, the return on average assets was 1.73%, the return on average equity was 14.70%, and the efficiency ratio was 56.2%.

Warner Bros. bidding war: JPMorgan, Allen & Co set to earn $180M in M&A fees
data.description:
JPMorgan (JPM) and Allen & Company stand to earn $90M each for serving as advisers to Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), regardless of which company wins the bidding war for the media giant, according to an exchange filing.

Warner Bros. (WBD) paid JPMorgan (JPM) about $189M in fees two years prior to the deal for financial advisory and other services.

This included JPMorgan's (JPM) work on a bridge loan that allowed Warner Bros. (WBD) to pursue its separation into two companies.

JPMorgan (JPM) worked with Warner Bros. (WBD) for over two years on possible M&A options and came up with the plan to split the company in two, a source told Reuters.

For this, Warner Bros. (WBD) bought back about half of its bonds at a discount, in a deal that was financed with a $17.5B bridge loan from JPMorgan (JPM). It's said to be the largest non-investment-grade bridge loan ever made on Wall Street.

Netflix (NFLX) sweetened its offer for Warner Bros.' (WBD) streaming and studio assets this week. Investors now await updates from Paramount Skydance (PSKY), whose tender offer for the entire company closes on Wednesday.

data.title: Denmark's Frederiksen says sovereignty is non-negotiable after Trump's threat - AP
Karman: Seemann, MSC deal to boost access to U.S. Navy programs, lift 2026 results
data.description:
Karman Holdings’ (KRMN) shares climbed ~9% in premarket trading on Thursday after the company lifted its fiscal 2025 guidance, forecasting revenue of $470 million to $471 million and adjusted EBITDA of $144.5 million to $144.9 million.

For fiscal year 2026, it expects revenue between $700 million and $715 million and adjusted EBITDA between $205 million and $215 million

Analysts estimate revenue of $464.01M for the year ending Dec 2025 and $577.65M for the following year. 

Furthermore, Karman Space & Defense said the Seemann and MSC transaction, valued at $220 million, is expected to expand its access to multi-decade, high-priority, funded U.S. Navy programs and to be immediately accretive in 2026 to revenue growth, funded backlog, EBITDA, earnings per share, and cash flow.

“Our core business continues to thrive as we build greater scale and capabilities through strategic acquisitions,” said Tony Koblinski, Karman (KRMN) chief executive officer.

“From deep sea to deep space, Seemann and MSC will make us truly an all-domain solutions provider with a larger addressable market and greater funding visibility and revenue across more of the nation’s highest priority programs.”

Karman further anticipates that the acquisition will support its position at the upper echelon of adjusted EBITDA margins among defense technology companies.

Source: Filing
